speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us about learning your trade, continuing your schooling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I came to Plzen. And short time after the concentration camp, I start to work and get my education in electromechanical engineering. And, so I worked part time. And part time, I went to school. Then my uncle was transferred to Olomouc, where I continued my education and worked. Then, in '48, when the communists start to tie down more their totalitarian [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] regime, I said this is not for me. [LAUGHS] And so I decided to leave Czechoslovakia. My-- that was in 1948, nearing '48. So I said, the best thing would be for to me disappear. And I joined a Jewish group, who were preparing to go to Israel. And this was my quickest opportunity to, to leave Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get a group [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of young people, mostly war orphans, who were survivors of concentration camp. And we will say, we go flight to Israel. So we were prepared near Brno. This is in Miroslav. We were prepared for a training, like a little bit military and little bit working in fields and so forth. And we were prepared for life in, in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [LAUGHS] one day, I, I came to Miroslav, and they told me there is a nice [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] blonde lady. [LAUGHS] That will be a girl you will like. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Here came Miriam. [SOBS] And this was when we met. And we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the situation got real bad by the communists. So we, we got some false documents and papers who talk-- who were speaking Hungarian, got to say was born in Hungary. And Miriam got, I think, some Polish papers. And we-- went to-- we crossed the Czechoslovakian border to Austria. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: On these false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the false passport. There was whole group, whole train of us. And we arrived to-- from Austria, we arrived to Venice. There we boarded on, on a ship. That was not a passenger luxury ship. We slept underneath. [LAUGHS] And the name of the ship was [ITALIAN], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Italian ship. And Miriam was very, very seasick. Well, she couldn't travel. So I had some nice cookies what that my aunt baked.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I feed her. [LAUGHS] Only the cookies were wasted. [LAUGHS] She couldn't keep them down. So after a few days on the ocean, we were interrupted by the English Army by Cypress, near Nicosia. They came and somehow there was all kinds of finagling with the English. They didn't want to let us into, to Israel, while they knew we will fight the English and also the, the Arabs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only by-- they came and said they are some different refugees. I don't know what story they found. And they continue-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] let us go. And then is a very short trip from Cyprus to Haifa. We landed in Haifa. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the girls, some of them went to army. Some of them went to navy. And Miriam and some girls went to kibbutz, what means this-- they work agricultural work. And this is a-- kibbutz is actually a commune. And she worked there. And I was right away [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] taken to the army, while there was still the independent war going on. And I was sworn in, by a fellow I remember. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was from the French Legion, from the-- and he told me, you know, if you, if you want survive in Holy Land, you pray. Only if you want survive, you have to do some shooting too. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were sworn in. And we got the training in army. And I, I had to my trade, so I right away was chosen to the Panzer, to the tank troops. And I was in charge of the-- all electromechanical department. And I was in command in, in my section. Only, if war was, everybody had to go and fight.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- when I was an army those three years, then the Israel was proclaimed. And we were together with the group, in the army, in same unit, what we came from Czechoslovakia. Only there were also different people trained. Some of them where in paratroopers. And some of them were in, in, in air force. Some were pilots. And there were some conflicts, when I was living in Israel. So we were always in some skirmish there and so forth. I served three years in the Army. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you in contact with Miriam this whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We visited each other. And Miriam was in kibbutz. And I, I was in Army. And then before I finished the army, my uncle, who give me the refuge after concentration camp, he had to run away from Czechoslovakia. And he couldn't come to United States. And they told him you have two choices. Or you have to finish your life, or we will put you in a nut house for protection for a few days, and you have to leave Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he left Czechoslovakia with my Aunt Elizabeth and the uncle. And they had a son, by name Milan. And-- the only son-- and they came to Czechoslovakia. And before I-- They left Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: They left Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Czechoslovakia and came to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, they didn't have the intention to stay. Well, they couldn't get to United States. So-- there was a quota. So they had to wait till their turn come in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So before I finished army, I was not the type where I could live a communal life. I was very independent fellow. And I says, I can't do things where people make the decision for me and so forth. So I says, I will go and-- go in town and wait. I always dreamed, like a child, come to United States and be with-- like my uncle who left, who left [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Czechoslovakia before-- that was in 1918, that was just before the end of World War I. And he came to New York. And I always dreamed to come to the United States, like a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I always, in back of my mind. And I worked on Miriam. I says, I want go to United States. And Miriam was a big Zionist. And she dreamed about this, sort of-- [LAUGHS] and so-- not from an allegiance standpoint, only from nationalistic standpoint. And she says, no way. You get me out of kibbutz, only you'll never get me out of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of Israel, to, to go to United States. And I tried very hardly [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to live in Israel. Only the land was small, and also the opportunity were small. And I felt this-- I can do much better than be-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was used to from home live a very nice life. And everything-- you didn't had to worry how much cost of what everything. I talking back--

speaker: interviewer
text: To your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: To my childhood and before. So today, the lifestyle is different than 50 or 70 years ago, when my parents live. Only on this conditions, they live a wonderful life, where sky was the limit. And there was not worry daily, worry from where the food will come. There was everything plentiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't visualize the life I can have Israel. Well, that was a young country. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was nothing. Everything was destroyed. So I says, this is very hard to achieve what I felt I can achieve. So Miriam went. Before I finished, we didn't had money. I didn't had nothing. So Miriam was very-- actually [LAUGHS] my aunt had a big vision and says, Miriam, you can-- [LAUGHS] you can do anything. Why don't you come out?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she worked in hospital, you know, a lady from an office, sort of like a cook. Had white gloves and everything, and here she had to cook for, for people in camp, where the people were coming in from Yemen. So she went and got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, where you and Miriam married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: At some point you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was May 4, 1940-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] one second, '50. And we got married. We didn't had where to-- where to-- she was still in kibbutz. Only we paid for our, for our wedding. Well, she knew she'd dare not stay anymore in kibbutz. And she was very, she didn't want take advantage, even so she put in three years of labor and not getting paid just for shirt and, and pants what she wore. And then we went on honeymoon. I got my sea rations for food. Well, we got very little money. And I got from the camp commander, who was my friend-- you know in the army it's not like you're playing soldier. You, you-- it's very big camaraderie. And if one have to jump into fire for the other, he does it regardless, thinking of his own life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he gave me a pass with, with motel like, for two weeks. And actually, I stayed the one week. And Miriam had the one week on the same pass. So we spent-- we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] spent the honeymoon by Lake of Galilee, I mean of the Sea of Galilee. And that was our honeymoon. And in the morning, we went and look how people have some, some, some scrambled eggs and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we just looked at it. We had some little bit of roll and sea rations. Only we were happy. And this how, how we spend the honeymoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: And started your live together in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we still didn't had-- head over the roof. Miriam left kibbutz just a few months before and started working like nurse, by the aunt, she work in hospital there, and she was cooking. She says Miriam, you can do anything, you're very handy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Says, what do you mean? You be a nurse. [LAUGHS] And said, I never, never, I couldn't give shots or anything. And people were dehydrated, so she had to go with long needle and inject fluid under the skin. She worked as a nurse in the refugee camp, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She worked like a nurse. She learned how to give shots. And then at this time, there was-- antibiotics had to be diluted, and it was not so easy. So she work like nurse. And this how she started and saved up money. And when I, when I finished the army, I didn't had a home. So Miriam come look for me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I checked out of-- I had only one night or two nights in, in like a motel, army motel. I checked out, and Miriam come look for me. He says, oh, he checked out. [LAUGHS] So she didn't know where to look for me. In the time being, I was running to, to looking for some work. And I found work right away. First I went to union, and they told me oh, this and that. So I didn't take the answer for no. And I went and I found a job. His name was Eisler. He was a Viennese Jewish fellow. And he had a business in Israel. And he asked me where I had training and so forth. I says, look here, give me a chance of one week, two week. And if I am good, you pay me. If I am not good-- and so I got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The money--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the job, what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Auto electrical.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Miriam, the money what we saved, we were looking-- and that was not easy to find-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an apartment. An apartment, what do you mean? There was a nice villa. Well, this was on the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: We got one room. And we had a electric cooker, so we can boil water to make coffee or tea in the room. And we shared the toilet and the shower with two other families. So this was, we had a roof over the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And had each other. When you're young, so you, you improvise a lot. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how long did you, did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, we live 12 years. We had two sons. One, Rafael, and one, Daniel was born in Israel. And then when Rafael was born, it was the first son. And then Daniel. And then in '67, I again went to war. And I was mobilized long time before. Well, we always, when was some skirmish or prepare for war, if you in tanks, and you specialize in-- so you always was mobilized the first. Prepared the equipment, and the last to leave to--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in the Suez. There was a very bloody war. We went all the way to Suez and participate in this war. That was a very messy war.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- that was a very, very bad experience. While I was in the shower and-- and before, when you were in the war and you didn't had kids, you didn't worry. You go and everything. Only this time, we-- I had my son. And you know, we were in groups. And they come with seconds of notice, not, no tomorrow notice or so forth. And I was in shower and says, come on, we have to go. And I got first time shivers in my life to, to, to continue in this war. In time being, I work on Miriam all the time. This, we have a hard life. And we did very well. Then I become-- I work for different company. And then I, on the end, I really was partner of a corporation, where we were a big outfit and I earned decent money and started to be good. Only I still had the feeling this, I can't unwind my, my energy and accomplish what I wanted to accomplish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that when you decided to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided to come to United States. And then the quota was much easier. And Miriam didn't believe we will get on the quota so fast. And one day, they give us a call to the, to the consulate. And we got papers to exit to United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you come to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to-- again, to the aunt, she left Israel. We came to Alhambra.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what, what year was that that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- 12 years later. That was '45, oh, '59. No, '69. Wait.

speaker: interviewee
text: '59. I came to Israel, to my-- to Alhambra, to United States. We came-- we landed in New York. From New York, we right away changed planes. And with two kids, I arrive to the United States. I was looking forward to it. And I, I always was happy. I was my dream. And when I came to landing to Los Angeles, and I saw the ocean of endless lights, [LAUGHS] I got scared. And I said, oh my god, I said, I will get lost in those, between those lights, here with two little kids. And we had to sell everything. 12 years of work, and I came with, with minus. I had to borrow still money, like $300 from different relatives. And later on, I paid them back. And we arrived to Los Angeles. And my aunt and uncle and my cousin were waiting for us. And we stayed with them, now I don't know, maybe a few weeks. And we-- I started to work right away. And I start to earn money. And, and we saved up money for the apartment. Well, when we-- we had difficultly to find-- we couldn't rent a house, why we didn't have the-- then I didn't have the earnings. So we rented a apartment. Only, nobody wanted to rent the apartment with two little kids. And we told them, the kids are good-mannered kids, you know. We live a very strict life, very organized life. They would, would rent us, nobody. Then finally a family, there were just then living just two families in Alhambra. And the family [NON-ENGLISH], they rented us finally a one-bedroom apartment. And the we-- the kids slept in the bedroom, and we sleep in living room. And I think so later on we rented the same place, bigger, two-bedroom apartment. And I was employed by name Bob Wandries in Alhambra. And I worked with Mercedes Benz. And, of course, I had experience with all the foreign cars.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was lucky. He-- also there was a hard time. There was a small recession. That was the time when Eisenhower was president. And there was a small recession, and there was hard time to find a job. So I again came with my aunt to Bob Wandries. And the manager was then John Rally. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the aunt told him, you need any mechanics who specialize in fuel injection or in diesel injection or in-- then that was still rare. While the American cars didn't had just the diesel, they didn't have the-- like today, the fuel injections. Then they were very fashionable, the gold wing Mercedes and so forth. So you found a good job? I found a good job. Also, they told me we don't need it. Only I says, well try me out and pay me what I am worth. And I was very good treated and-- Did you also, did your family continue to grow?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family start to grow with the prosperity. And we-- at that, Alexander was born. And then later on, I went and worked for a shop. I quit my job, and I continue and I work and become a partner. And from there on, we grow in business. I enlarged the business. My partner was American-born. His name was Harold Borden. And he also had an engineering degree and later on also in psychology a degree. He graduated from Cal State LA. And we had a very good going business, in a very nice community in Alhambra. And we-- I meet lots of people, mostly people were from different backgrounds. There were Spanish, Italian. And, and they support me very much and help me to, to grow. And then I did some other investment, where I start to be more successful. And the kids continued to schools. They went to great school, what was very good. And then they went to City College.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did you, during this time at all, did you ever talk about your experiences during the war to any of your new friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They knew. They asked me, so I told them. Only we didn't, didn't disclose. Matter of fact, I had, believe it or not, I-- we befriended a very nice family, and they were Germans. And matter of fact, Miriam and their kids, like small kids, they, they played together, and we celebrated things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then even, they left the United States. And we visited them in Germany. They were living in, in-- what do they call it? Where the Peter Piper-- Hamelin. We visited them several times.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had friends from all walks of life. Like we have here friends, two friends, they are doctors and from all walks of life.
